title: Capital Without Category
text:
Welcome
This website presents our project on governance, inequality, and the micro-enterprise paradox in Brazil.
Use the sidebar to navigate through our EDA, written analysis, and appendix materials.

title: Capital Without Category — Part I
text:
Background
Brazil’s official firm-size classification (Simples Nacional) is built on a single threshold: annual gross revenue. A sole proprietor earning 360,000 BRL is legally a “micro-enterprise” regardless of whether their declared capital stock is 50,000 BRL or 5,000,000 BRL. This regulatory choice creates a natural experiment in the CNPJ registry: firms within the same legal category can have wildly different capital endowments.
Earlier coverage of this dataset reported high Gini coefficients but reported them at the aggregate level, leaving open a question central to policy design: is the extreme concentration a micro-enterprise pathology — i.e., a problem specific to the smallest firms — or a universal property of the Brazilian formal sector?
The answer matters. If concentration is concentrated in micros, raising thresholds or splitting micros into more granular tiers could bring meaningful redistribution. If concentration is universal, threshold reform is cosmetic — the same power-law sits behind every regulatory label, and revenue is the wrong targeting dimension.

title: Capital Without Category — Part I
text:

Q1. Is capital concentration in Brazilian formal-sector firms a feature of the micro-enterprise category specifically, or is the same power-law signature visible across every size tier?

Design Conventions


Show code
library(tidyverse)
library(scales)
library(patchwork)
library(boot)        # bootstrap Gini CIs
library(gt)          # publication-quality tables

# ── Shared palette ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
pal_size <- c(
  "Micro"        = "#c3553a",   # terracotta
  "Small"        = "#d9a441",   # amber
  "Medium/Large" = "#2a5a6b"    # deep teal
)

pal_segment <- c(
  "Top 1%"     = "#1d3557",
  "Next 9%"    = "#457b9d",
  "Next 40%"   = "#a8dadc",
  "Bottom 50%" = "#e9ecef"
)

# ── Shared theme ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
theme_cwc <- function(base_size = 13) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(
      plot.title        = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size + 2,
                                       colour = "#1d3557", margin = margin(b = 4)),
      plot.subtitle     = element_text(colour = "grey35", size = base_size - 2,
                                       lineheight = 1.35, margin = margin(b = 18)),
      plot.caption      = element_text(colour = "grey55", size = base_size - 5,
                                       hjust = 0, margin = margin(t = 12)),
      plot.title.position   = "plot",
      plot.caption.position = "plot",
      strip.text        = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size - 1,
                                       colour = "#1d3557"),
      strip.background  = element_rect(fill = "grey96", colour = NA),
      panel.grid.minor  = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major  = element_line(colour = "grey92", linewidth = 0.3),
      panel.spacing     = unit(1.4, "lines"),
      legend.position   = "top",
      legend.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size - 2),
      legend.text       = element_text(size = base_size - 2),
      axis.title        = element_text(colour = "grey30", size = base_size - 2),
      axis.text         = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = base_size - 3)
    )
}

caption_text <- paste(
  "Source: Brazilian CNPJ Registry · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 4 (Extended)",
  "· Boyd, He & Miao · 2026-02-25"
)

Data Preparation

companies <- read_csv(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2026/2026-01-27/companies.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

co <- companies |>
  filter(
    !is.na(company_size),
    !is.na(capital_stock),
    !is.na(owner_qualification),
    capital_stock > 0
  ) |>
  mutate(
    size = case_when(
      company_size == "micro-enterprise" ~ "Micro",
      company_size == "small-enterprise" ~ "Small",
      TRUE                               ~ "Medium/Large"
    ),
    size = factor(size, levels = c("Micro", "Small", "Medium/Large")),
    log_capital = log10(capital_stock)

title: Capital Without Category — Part I
section: Analysis: Concentration is Universal Across Tiers
text:
Analysis: Concentration is Universal Across Tiers

Methodological motivation
Plain Lorenz curves are a poor visualization tool when inequality is extreme. When the top 1% of firms holds something like 96–99% of capital, three Lorenz curves at Gini 0.98, 0.99, 0.995 plot as nearly identical near-L shapes — visual indistinguishability where the analytically interesting separation actually lives. Two adjustments make the structure readable:

Log-rank concentration curves. Plot cumulative share of capital against the log of rank percentile (top 0.1%, top 1%, top 10%, top 50%). Logarithmic rescaling on the rank axis expands the upper tail where the dispersion across tiers is concentrated. What looks like a single curve on linear scale resolves into separate curves once the top decile is given its proper visual weight.
Stacked rank-bucket decomposition. Convert the Gini coefficient into a directly readable percentage by reporting the share of each tier’s capital held by the top 1%, next 9%, next 40%, and bottom 50%. This is harder to misread than Gini and communicates immediately to non-technical audiences.

We also report bootstrap 95% confidence intervals on the Gini point estimates (B = 1,000 resamples per tier) to confirm that the small numerical differences between tier Ginis are not artifacts of sample variance. With N at this scale, the CIs are mechanically tight — but reporting them is good discipline.


Bootstrap Gini estimation


Show code
gini <- function(x) {
  x <- sort(x[!is.na(x) & x > 0])
  n <- length(x)
  if (n < 2L) return(NA_real_)
  2 * sum(seq_len(n) * x) / (n * sum(x)) - (n + 1) / n
}

gini_stat <- function(data, i) gini(data[i])

set.seed(2026)
gini_ci <- co |>
  group_by(size) |>
  group_modify(~ {
    b  <- boot(.x$capital_stock, gini_stat, R = 1000)
    ci <- boot.ci(b, type = "perc", conf = 0.95)
    tibble(
      g     = b$t0,
      lower = ci$percent[4L],
      upper = ci$percent[5L],
      n     = nrow(.x)
    )
  }) |>
  ungroup()

# Top-1% share — robust complement to Gini in extreme-tail regimes
top1_share <- co |>
  group_by(size) |>
  arrange(desc(capital_stock), .by_group = TRUE) |>
  summarise(
    top1 = sum(capital_stock[seq_len(ceiling(n() * 0.01))]) / sum(capital_stock),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

tier_stats <- gini_ci |>
  left_join(top1_share, by = "size") |>
  mutate(
    label = sprintf("%-13s  Gini %.3f  [%.3f, %.3f]   Top 1%% holds %s",
                    size, g, lower, upper,
                    percent(top1, accuracy = 1))
  )

gini_ci |>
  left_join(top1_share, by = "size") |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Bootstrapped Gini coefficients and top-1% capital shares**"),
    subtitle = "B = 1,000 percentile bootstrap resamples per tier"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    size = "Tier", g = "Gini",
    lower = "CI Lower", upper = "CI Upper",
    n = "N", top1 = "Top 1% share"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(g, lower, upper), decimals = 4) |>
  fmt_percent(columns = top1, decimals = 1) |>
  fmt_number(columns = n, use_seps = TRUE, decimals = 0) |>
  tab_options(table.font.size = px(13), heading.align = "left")

Two-panel concentration figure


Show code
# ── Panel A: log-rank concentration curve ───────────────────────────────────
conc_curve <- co |>
  group_by(size) |>
  arrange(desc(capital_stock), .by_group = TRUE) |>
  mutate(
    rank_pct = row_number() / n(),
    cum_top  = cumsum(capital_stock) / sum(capital_stock)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(rank_pct <= 0.5)

p_conc <- ggplot(conc_curve, aes(x = rank_pct, y = cum_top, colour = size)) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0.01, xend = 0.01, y = 0, yend = 1,
           colour = "grey75", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.45) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.01, y = 1.04, label = "Top 1%",
           colour = "grey40", size = 3.3, fontface = "italic") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.5, alpha = 0.92) +
  scale_x_log10(
    breaks = c(0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 0.5),
    labels = c("Top 0.1%", "Top 1%", "Top 10%", "Top 50%"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.05))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(),
    limits = c(0, 1.08),
    expand = c(0, 0),
    name   = "Cumulative share of tier's capital"
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = pal_size,
                      labels = tier_stats$label,
                      name   = NULL) +
  labs(
    x = "Rank of firms (log scale, highest capital first) →",
    subtitle = "(A)  Concentration curve — log-rank x-axis reveals the\n        tier separation that linear Lorenz cannot show"
  ) +
  theme_cwc() +
  theme(
    legend.position      = c(0.02, 0.98),
    legend.justification = c(0, 1),
    legend.background    = element_rect(fill = alpha("white", 0.9), colour = NA),
    legend.key.width     = unit(1.5, "lines"),
    legend.text          = element_text(family = "mono", size = 9),
    plot.subtitle        = element_text(colour = "grey35", size = 11,
                                        lineheight = 1.3, margin = margin(b = 10))
  )

# ── Panel B: stacked rank-bucket decomposition ──────────────────────────────
decomp <- co |>
  group_by(size) |>
  arrange(desc(capital_stock), .by_group = TRUE) |>
  mutate(
    rank_pct = row_number() / n(),
    segment  = case_when(
      rank_pct <= 0.01 ~ "Top 1%",
      rank_pct <= 0.10 ~ "Next 9%",
      rank_pct <= 0.50 ~ "Next 40%",
      TRUE             ~ "Bottom 50%"
    ),
    segment  = factor(segment,
                      levels = c("Bottom 50%", "Next 40%", "Next 9%", "Top 1%"))
  ) |>
  group_by(size, segment) |>
  summarise(cap = sum(capital_stock), .groups = "drop") |>
  group_by(size) |>
  mutate(share = cap / sum(cap)) |>
  ungroup()

p_decomp <- ggplot(decomp, aes(x = size, y = share, fill = segment)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.62, colour = "white", linewidth = 1.4) +
  geom_text(
    data = filter(decomp, share >= 0.04),
    aes(label = percent(share, accuracy = 1),
        colour = segment %in% c("Top 1%", "Next 9%")),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
    fontface = "bold", size = 4, show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = pal_segment,
    breaks = c("Top 1%", "Next 9%", "Next 40%", "Bottom 50%"),
    name   = NULL
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "white", `FALSE` = "grey25")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(),
                     expand = c(0, 0),
                     name   = "Share of tier's total capital") +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    subtitle = "(B)  Each tier's top 1% holds ~96–99% of the tier's\n        capital. The bottom half is a sliver."
  ) +
  theme_cwc() +
  theme(
    legend.position    = "top",
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    plot.subtitle      = element_text(colour = "grey35", size = 11,
                                      lineheight = 1.3, margin = margin(b = 10))
  )

# ── Combine ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
(p_conc | p_decomp) +
  plot_layout(widths = c(1.15, 1)) +
  plot_annotation(
    title    = "Capital concentration is near-total in every Brazilian firm-size tier",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Gini coefficients exceed 0.98 across Micro, Small, and Medium/Large — ",
      "'micro-enterprise' is not uniquely unequal.\n",
      "The capital trap is a universal power-law structure, not a feature of small firms."
    ),
    caption  = caption_text,
    theme    = theme_cwc()
  ) &
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16, colour = "#1d3557"))








Figure 2.1: Capital concentration is near-total in every Brazilian firm-size tier.







Interpretation
Three findings emerge.
The Ginis are statistically distinguishable but economically negligible. All three tier Ginis exceed 0.98, with bootstrap 95% CIs that are tight and non-overlapping. Statistically the tiers differ; substantively all three are pinned against the ceiling of the Gini scale. No reasonable inequality target distinguishes 0.985 from 0.992.
Top-1% capital share is the dominant fact. In each tier, the top one percent of firms holds 96–99% of that tier’s capital. The bottom 50% holds less than one percent. This is not “high inequality” in the colloquial sense; it is a power-law in which nearly all economic mass sits at the top of the distribution.
The log-rank view rescues information that linear Lorenz hides. Panel A shows that the three tiers are not visually identical when the rank axis is log-scaled — Micro firms reach 80% cumulative share faster than Medium/Large, indicating that the shape of the tail differs across tiers even when the aggregate Gini is similar. Linear Lorenz curves cannot show this because the linear x-axis crushes the upper decile into 10% of the canvas.
The “capital trap” narrative attached to micro-enterprises in earlier framings is misplaced. The concentration is structural to the entire Brazilian formal sector, not specific to the micro tier. This reframes the analytical agenda: if the inequality shape is universal, the substantive question is no longer which tier is unequal but who occupies the top of each tier — and that question cannot be answered by size classification. It requires governance information.

title: Capital Without Category — Part I
text:
Methodological Notes

Gini stability at extreme concentration. When the top firms dominate, Gini becomes mechanically close to 1 and economically meaningful changes are compressed into the third decimal. Top-1% share is more interpretable in this regime and invariant to the lower-tail definition.
Bootstrap sample size. With N > 100,000 per tier, B = 1,000 percentile bootstrap resamples produce CIs in the fourth decimal place. We report them as a discipline against assuming a single resampling could change the qualitative finding, not because they add new information.
Excluding zero / missing capital. Reported zero capital often reflects registry-coding artifacts (entities awaiting capital declaration after registration) rather than genuinely zero-capital operating firms. Including zeros would inflate the bottom-share estimates artifactually and pull the Lorenz curve toward exact-L shape.
Why not Theil decomposition? A Theil-T within-vs-between decomposition is a natural next step, and earlier drafts of this analysis included one. We dropped it here because the universal-power-law finding makes the between-tier component uninformative: when every tier has Gini > 0.98, decomposing tells us only that within-tier inequality dominates, which we already know visually. The decomposition is a useful exercise when between-tier differences carry information; here, they don’t.
crumbs: 2  Capital Without Category — Part I

title: Capital Without Category — Part I
text:
Forward Link
If concentration is universal but the question is who sits at the top of each tier, the analytical next step is to ask whether governance archetype — the legal role of the controlling person — predicts capital independently of size. Part II takes that question, examining whether governance shifts the level of capital, the variance of capital, or both.
crumbs: 2  Capital Without Category — Part I

title: Capital Without Category — Part II
text:
Background
Part I established that capital concentration in Brazilian formal-sector firms is a universal property: every firm-size tier exhibits Gini > 0.98 and a top-1% capital share of 96–99%. The shape of the distribution is the same across regulatory categories. What differs across tiers is who occupies the top — and the firm-size classification cannot answer that question, because it is built on annual revenue and is silent on the legal architecture of the controlling party.
The CNPJ registry’s owner_qualification field carries that missing information. It records the legal role of the controlling person under three broad archetypes:
These archetypes map onto well-established predictions from agency theory. Sole Entrepreneurs face the highest information asymmetries with external lenders and have no separation between personal and business liability. Managing Partners signal active human capital and shared risk-bearing, lowering the information cost of credit. Beneficial Owners are heterogeneous: at the small end they are dormant shells; at the large end they are investment vehicles channelling capital from institutional or family-office structures.
crumbs: 3  Capital Without Category — Part II

title: Capital Without Category — Part II
text:
Sole Entrepreneur — a natural person operating under their own name, fully exposed to business risk.
Managing Partner / Administrator — an active operator who runs the business as one of one or more named partners with operational authority.
Beneficial Owner (passive / investor) — a controlling equity holder who may sit behind a holding-company chain, often without operational involvement.
crumbs: 3  Capital Without Category — Part II

title: Capital Without Category — Part II
text:
Research Question

Q2. Conditional on firm size, does governance archetype shape the capital distribution? If it does, is the effect a level shift (governance moves the median), a variance shift (governance moves the tail), or both?

The question matters because the answer dictates the targeting strategy. A level shift means governance acts as a coarse credit-access proxy. A variance shift means governance is selecting different populations of firms — and lumping them by size will systematically misallocate any size-targeted policy.

title: Capital Without Category — Part II
text:
Data Preparation


Show code
companies <- read_csv(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2026/2026-01-27/companies.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

co <- companies |>
  filter(
    !is.na(company_size),
    !is.na(capital_stock),
    !is.na(owner_qualification),
    capital_stock > 0
  ) |>
  mutate(
    size = case_when(
      company_size == "micro-enterprise" ~ "Micro",
      company_size == "small-enterprise" ~ "Small",
      TRUE                               ~ "Medium/Large"
    ),
    size = factor(size, levels = c("Micro", "Small", "Medium/Large")),

    governance = case_when(
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("beneficial owner", ignore_case = TRUE))     ~ "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)",
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("entrepreneur|business owner", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Sole Entrepreneur",
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("managing partner|administrator", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    governance = factor(governance, levels = c(
      "Sole Entrepreneur",
      "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)",
      "Other"
    )),

    log_capital = log10(capital_stock)
  )

cat("Observations:", comma(nrow(co)), "\n")

title: Capital Without Category — Part II
text:
Descriptive Snapshot: Governance × Size Cells
The table below carries the data plotted in the two-panel figure that follows. Two columns matter most: the median, which gives the level summary that Panel A tracks across tiers, and the mean / median ratio, which gives the tail-heaviness summary that Panel B tracks. Ratios close to 1 signal a roughly symmetric distribution; ratios in the double or triple digits signal a heavy right tail. As we will see, both moments tell tier-dependent stories rather than uniform archetype-level ones.


Show code
gov_cells <- co |>
  filter(governance != "Other") |>
  group_by(size, governance) |>
  summarise(
    n         = n(),
    median    = median(capital_stock),
    mean      = mean(capital_stock),
    p90       = quantile(capital_stock, 0.90),
    mean_med  = mean / median,
    .groups   = "drop"
  )

gov_cells |>
  mutate(governance = str_replace(governance, "\n", " ")) |>
  gt(groupname_col = "size") |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Capital stock by size × governance**"),
    subtitle = "Mean / median ratio diagnoses tail heaviness within each cell"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    governance = "Governance archetype",
    n = "N", median = "Median", mean = "Mean",
    p90 = "P90", mean_med = "Mean / Median"
  ) |>

title: Capital Without Category — Part II
section: Analysis: Governance Shapes Distributions, Not Just Levels
text:
Analysis: Governance Shapes Distributions, Not Just Levels

Methodological motivation
A boxplot or a bar chart of medians would already show that governance archetypes differ on average — but it would miss the analytically critical question: do they differ in level (a translation of the entire distribution) or in shape (different variances and tail heaviness)? Two complementary panels surface both moments at once:

Panel A — median trajectories with range. For each governance archetype, plot median capital across size tiers, with the middle 80% of the distribution (P10–P90) shown as thin whiskers and the IQR (P25–P75) as a thicker translucent bar. Connecting medians across tiers within an archetype reveals whether that archetype’s level scales with size or stays flat.
Panel B — mean ÷ median ratio. The gap between mean and median is a robust tail-heaviness diagnostic: when the right tail is heavy, the mean is pulled far above the median and the ratio diverges from 1. Plotting this ratio on a log scale by size tier surfaces tail behaviour that the median trajectory in Panel A cannot show directly.

Why not density ridges? An earlier draft used geom_density_ridges to show full distributional shape side-by-side. Ridges work well when distributions are roughly comparable in scale, but capital stock is so heavy-tailed that the ridge for each cell collapses into a thin spike at the lower end with a faint right-trailing arm — visually, all nine cells look nearly identical even when their statistical moments differ by orders of magnitude. The trajectory + ratio design isolates the two moments that actually carry the story.
For inferential support we use the Kruskal-Wallis test within each size tier. This is a non-parametric one-way analysis of variance on ranks: appropriate here because capital-stock distributions are extremely log-normal at best and have heavy right tails that violate ANOVA’s normality assumption. Kruskal-Wallis tests the null hypothesis that all governance subgroups within a size tier are drawn from the same distribution. Rejecting the null does not by itself say what differs — that interpretation comes from the visual.


Kruskal-Wallis tests within each size tier


Show code
kw_results <- co |>
  filter(governance != "Other") |>
  group_by(size) |>
  summarise(
    statistic = kruskal.test(capital_stock ~ governance)$statistic,
    df        = kruskal.test(capital_stock ~ governance)$parameter,
    p_value   = kruskal.test(capital_stock ~ governance)$p.value,
    n         = n(),
    .groups   = "drop"
  )

kw_results |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Kruskal-Wallis tests of governance × capital within each tier**"),
    subtitle = "H₀: all three governance subgroups draw from the same capital distribution"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    size = "Tier", statistic = "H statistic",
    df = "df", p_value = "p-value", n = "N"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(statistic), decimals = 1) |>
  fmt_number(columns = n, use_seps = TRUE, decimals = 0) |>
  fmt_scientific(columns = p_value, decimals = 2) |>
  tab_options(table.font.size = px(13), heading.align = "left")

The null is rejected at conventional levels in every tier. Governance and capital are not independent within any size category. The visual now supplies the what.


Two-panel figure: median trajectories + tail-heaviness ratio


Show code
# ── Per-cell summary statistics ─────────────────────────────────────────────
gov_summary <- co |>
  filter(governance != "Other") |>
  group_by(size, governance) |>
  summarise(
    p10    = quantile(capital_stock, 0.10),
    p25    = quantile(capital_stock, 0.25),
    median = median(capital_stock),
    mean   = mean(capital_stock),
    p75    = quantile(capital_stock, 0.75),
    p90    = quantile(capital_stock, 0.90),
    n      = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    mean_med  = mean / median,
    gov_short = case_when(
      str_detect(governance, "Beneficial") ~ "Beneficial Owner",
      str_detect(governance, "Managing")   ~ "Managing Partner",
      TRUE                                  ~ "Sole Entrepreneur"
    ),
    gov_short = factor(gov_short, levels = c(
      "Sole Entrepreneur", "Managing Partner", "Beneficial Owner"
    ))
  )

pal_gov_short <- c(
  "Sole Entrepreneur" = "#d9a441",
  "Managing Partner"  = "#5b8a9a",
  "Beneficial Owner"  = "#1d3557"
)

dodge_w <- 0.45

# ── Panel A: median trajectories with IQR + P10–P90 whiskers ────────────────
p_level <- ggplot(
  gov_summary,
  aes(x = size, y = median, colour = gov_short, group = gov_short)
) +
  geom_linerange(aes(ymin = p10, ymax = p90),
                 linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.50,
                 position = position_dodge(width = dodge_w)) +
  geom_linerange(aes(ymin = p25, ymax = p75),
                 linewidth = 4.2, alpha = 0.30,
                 position = position_dodge(width = dodge_w)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.3, alpha = 0.85,
            position = position_dodge(width = dodge_w)) +
  geom_point(size = 5, shape = 21, fill = "white", stroke = 2,
             position = position_dodge(width = dodge_w)) +
  scale_y_log10(
    labels = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale(), accuracy = 1),
    breaks = c(1e5, 3e5, 1e6, 3e6, 1e7),
    name   = "Capital stock (BRL, log scale)"
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = pal_gov_short, name = NULL,
    guide  = guide_legend(override.aes = list(linewidth = 1.5, size = 4))
  ) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    subtitle = "(A)  Median capital with IQR (thick) and P10–P90 (thin)\n        Only Managing Partners scale upward across tiers"
  ) +
  theme_cwc() +
  theme(
    legend.position    = "top",
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    plot.subtitle      = element_text(colour = "grey35", size = 11,
                                      lineheight = 1.3, margin = margin(b = 10))
  )

# ── Panel B: mean ÷ median ratio (tail heaviness) ──────────────────────────
p_tail <- ggplot(
  gov_summary,
  aes(x = size, y = mean_med, colour = gov_short, group = gov_short)
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linewidth = 0.4,
             colour = "grey70", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.3, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_point(size = 5, shape = 21, fill = "white", stroke = 2) +
  # Label only the rightmost (Medium/Large) endpoint of each line
  # — avoids label collision at Micro/Small where Sole Entrepreneur and
  # Managing Partner ratios are similar
  geom_text(
    data = filter(gov_summary, size == "Medium/Large"),
    aes(label = paste0(round(mean_med), "×")),
    hjust = -0.4, fontface = "bold", size = 4.2, show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  scale_y_log10(
    breaks = c(1, 3, 10, 30, 100),
    labels = function(x) paste0(x, "×"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.22))
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.10, 0.22))) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = pal_gov_short, guide = "none") +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "Mean ÷ median  (tail heaviness)",
    subtitle = "(B)  Mean-to-median ratio\n        Tails are heavy in every cell, but the carrier shifts across tiers"
  ) +
  theme_cwc() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    plot.subtitle      = element_text(colour = "grey35", size = 11,
                                      lineheight = 1.3, margin = margin(b = 10)),
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 25, 10, 10)
  )

# ── Combine ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
(p_level | p_tail) +
  plot_layout(widths = c(1.4, 1), guides = "collect") +
  plot_annotation(
    title    = "Managing Partners scale the median; capital tails migrate across archetypes",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Capital distribution by owner archetype within each firm-size tier. ",
      "Panel A: median trajectory with middle 80% range.\n",
      "Panel B: mean ÷ median ratio captures tail heaviness — BO carries the tail at Micro; MP carries it at Medium/Large."
    ),
    caption  = caption_text,
    theme    = theme_cwc()
  ) &
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16, colour = "#1d3557"),
    legend.position = "top"
  )








Figure 3.1: Governance shapes the capital distribution differently across archetypes.







Interpretation
Panel A’s three trajectories give the level story:

Managing Partners are the only archetype whose median materially scales with size: 300k → 360k → 1M (a 3.3× increase from Micro to Medium/Large). Active operators accumulate capital as firms grow — capital and operational scale move together.
Sole Entrepreneurs show a flat median across Micro and Small (~300k), with a modest uptick at Medium/Large (~394k). Solo proprietors face information asymmetries that cap external capital access; revenue scale does not translate into capital in the same way.
Beneficial Owners stay flat at 300k throughout — for this archetype, the median is uninformative about firm size.

Panel B’s mean ÷ median ratios complicate the simple “Beneficial Owners carry the tail” story we initially expected. Capital distributions are heavy-tailed in nearly every cell (most ratios fall between 30× and 500×), but which archetype carries the heaviest tail changes with firm size:

At Micro, Beneficial Owners share the heaviest tail with Managing Partners (~80× each), while Sole Entrepreneurs are the lightest (~30×). This is the “capital-rich micro” pattern from the project framing: most BO micros are tiny, but a holding-company minority hold enormous capital.
At Small, the pattern reorganizes. Managing Partners’ tail grows sharply (~300×), Beneficial Owners’ tail moderates (~50×), and Sole Entrepreneurs’ tail collapses to near-symmetry (~2×). The Small tier appears to discipline capital distributions — possibly because Brazil’s revenue-band classifications create selection pressures at the boundaries.
At Medium/Large, Managing Partners reach an extraordinary 526× ratio, Sole Entrepreneurs a 127× ratio, and Beneficial Owners drop to 3× — the lightest tail in the entire panel. The largest active-management firms include genuine industry titans whose capital dwarfs the typical MP firm. Beneficial Owner firms classified as Medium/Large are, by contrast, a much more uniform population — likely dominated by institutional investment vehicles rather than the bimodal small-shell + mega-vehicle distribution found at Micro.

The structural finding is richer than a uniform “BO heavy-tail” story:

Capital-tail composition migrates across archetypes as firms scale. At the small end, the tail belongs to passive ownership (holding-company shells using BO governance). At the large end, the tail belongs to active management (true industry champions). The two heavy tails point to two structurally different phenomena that should not be conflated.
The “capital-rich micro” finding is specifically a Micro × Beneficial Owner phenomenon. It does not generalize to other tiers — and the heavy-tailed inequality at Medium/Large is a different question (extreme inequality among genuine large firms) that would need its own analytical frame.
Sole Entrepreneurs are not flat in the strong sense. Their median is roughly flat, but their tail is volatile — collapsing at Small and resurging at Medium/Large. The V-shape may reflect regulatory-boundary effects of Brazil’s revenue classifications, where firms strategically position themselves at specific capital levels to access (or avoid) tier-specific tax treatments.

The governance premium is not uniform along any single axis. At each tier it has a different shape, and any size-targeted policy that treats these archetypes as equivalent within a tier will systematically misallocate. The next step — Part III — zooms in on the Micro tier specifically, where the BO-tail phenomenon is sharpest, to identify the legal-form channel through which capital-rich micros achieve their unusual endowment.

title: Capital Without Category — Part II
text:
Methodological Notes

Why Kruskal-Wallis rather than ANOVA. Capital-stock distributions are strongly right-skewed even after log transformation; ANOVA’s normality assumption is violated. Kruskal-Wallis operates on ranks and is robust to this.
Why a two-panel design rather than ridges. Ridge plots show the full distribution shape, which is appealing in principle. In practice, capital-stock distributions are so heavy-tailed that the ridge for each cell collapses into a near-spike at the lower end, with the upper tail rendered as a faint trailing arm at any reasonable bandwidth. All nine size × governance cells then look visually similar even when their statistical moments differ by orders of magnitude. The trajectory + tail-ratio decomposition isolates the two moments (level, tail) that actually carry the analytical story.
Why mean-to-median ratio as the tail summary. The ratio is scale-invariant (insensitive to currency unit), bounded below by 1 for symmetric distributions, and easy to read as “the mean is N times the median”. Alternatives like skewness or kurtosis are mathematically equivalent in spirit but harder for non-statistical readers to interpret directly.
Sensitivity of the ratio to extreme values. The mean is by construction pulled by upper-tail outliers, which makes the ratio a combined signal of tail heaviness and outlier presence. At Medium/Large, where each governance cell contains thousands rather than hundreds of thousands of firms, a small number of mega-corporations can move the ratio dramatically. The 526× value for Managing Partners at Medium/Large reflects a real population-level fact (a handful of firms with capital in the hundreds of millions of BRL), but its precise magnitude could shift across years or registry vintages. The qualitative ranking — MP heaviest, BO lightest at Medium/Large — is robust; the specific multiples should be read as order-of-magnitude indicators rather than precise estimates.
Excluding “Other” governance. A residual category with heterogeneous coding (state-owned representatives, foreign legal entities, etc.) is excluded from the figure to keep archetype-level interpretation clean. It is approximately 6% of observations.
Why we dropped quantile regression here. An earlier draft estimated τ ∈ {0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.90} regressions. The two-panel design communicates the same finding (premium widens at the upper tail) more directly and without the rank-conditioned interpretation that quantile regression imposes. The distributional shape is the result.
crumbs: 3  Capital Without Category — Part II

title: Capital Without Category — Part II
text:
Forward Link
The most policy-relevant finding from this analysis is the Micro × Beneficial Owner cell: at the smallest tier, Beneficial Owners hold the heaviest tail, which is the statistical fingerprint of the “capital-rich micro” phenomenon that motivates this project. The development-policy question that follows is which legal-form channel turns that ownership pattern into a registered micro-enterprise with above-tier-typical capital. Part III ranks the legal-form × governance cells inside the micro tier and identifies the partnership-with-passive-equity structure as the dominant escape route — a result that overturns the popular “EIRELI-as-escape-route” framing. The Medium/Large tail behaviour (where Managing Partners reach a 526× mean/median ratio) is a different empirical phenomenon — extreme inequality among genuine large operating firms — and is not pursued further here, since it lies outside the small-business policy remit of this trilogy.
crumbs: 3  Capital Without Category — Part II

title: Capital Without Category — Part III
text:
Background
Parts I and II established two facts. First, capital concentration in Brazilian formal-sector firms is universal across regulatory tiers — every firm-size category has Gini > 0.98 and a top-1% capital share above 96%. Second, governance archetype shapes the capital distribution in distributional terms: Managing Partners lead the median (and scale with size), Sole Entrepreneurs stay flat, and Beneficial Owners carry a heavy upper tail at every size.
The combined picture leaves a sharp residual question. Inside the micro-enterprise tier — where Brazil’s main small-business support programs are targeted — there is a non-trivial subpopulation of firms with capital stock far above what their revenue classification predicts. These are firms whose annual gross revenue is below the 360,000 BRL Simples Nacional threshold but whose declared capital is above the 75th percentile of all micros. They are capital-rich micros: they wear the regulatory clothing of a small business but hold the capital stock of something larger.
If the goal of micro-enterprise policy is to support genuinely capital-constrained operators, capital-rich micros are a targeting failure: any subsidy or simplified- tax treatment they receive flows to firms that did not need it. The empirical question is what legal-structural signature these firms share. If a single legal form (say, EIRELI — Brazil’s individual limited-liability vehicle) accounts for most of them, the policy fix is straightforward: condition support on legal form in addition to revenue. If the signature is more textured — a specific combination of legal form and governance archetype — the fix has to be designed around that combination.
crumbs: 4  Capital Without Category — Part III

title: Capital Without Category — Part III
text:
Research Question

Q3. Among micro-enterprises, which combinations of legal form and governance archetype produce the highest share of capital-rich firms? Is the escape route a single legal form (the popular “EIRELI” framing), or a structural combination that no single dimension captures?

We define a capital-rich micro as a micro-enterprise whose capital stock falls in the top quartile of all micros’ capital — i.e., above the 75th percentile, which in this dataset is approximately BRL 500,000. This threshold is descriptive, not normative; it captures the upper tail of the micro distribution where “escape from the typical micro profile” begins to be analytically meaningful.

library(tidyverse)

text:
Data Preparation


Show code
companies <- read_csv(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2026/2026-01-27/companies.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

co <- companies |>
  filter(
    !is.na(company_size),
    !is.na(capital_stock),
    !is.na(owner_qualification),
    capital_stock > 0
  ) |>
  mutate(
    size = case_when(
      company_size == "micro-enterprise" ~ "Micro",
      company_size == "small-enterprise" ~ "Small",
      TRUE                               ~ "Medium/Large"
    ),
    size = factor(size, levels = c("Micro", "Small", "Medium/Large")),

    governance = case_when(
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("beneficial owner", ignore_case = TRUE))     ~ "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)",
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("entrepreneur|business owner", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Sole Entrepreneur",
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("managing partner|administrator", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    governance = factor(governance, levels = c(
      "Sole Entrepreneur",
      "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)",
      "Other"
    )),

    log_capital = log10(capital_stock),

    # Capital-rich threshold = 75th percentile within micros
    capital_q75_micro     = quantile(capital_stock[size == "Micro"], 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    is_capital_rich_micro = (size == "Micro") & (capital_stock >= capital_q75_micro)
  )

cat("Observations:", comma(nrow(co)), "\n")

title: Capital Without Category — Part III
section: Analysis: The Escape Route is Partnership-with-Passive-Equity
text:
Analysis: The Escape Route is Partnership-with-Passive-Equity

Methodological motivation
The simplest analytical approach — comparing escape rates by legal form alone — would obscure the answer, because legal form and governance are correlated but not identical. A Sole Proprietorship is mechanically tied to a Sole Entrepreneur (one operator, one identity), but an LLC or a Simple Partnership can be controlled by either an active Managing Partner or a passive Beneficial Owner. Collapsing across governance averages over these very different ownership mechanics.
We therefore compute escape rates at the legal-form × governance cell level within the micro tier. Two methodological details support credibility:

Cell-size threshold. We exclude any cell with fewer than 20 observations. With small n, sample-proportion estimates are noisy, and the visualization would be dominated by extreme rates from cells of n = 3 or n = 5. The cutoff is conservative; a sensitivity check at n ≥ 50 produces the same ranking.
Wilson 95% confidence intervals. For binomial proportions, the textbook normal-approximation (Wald) interval performs poorly when p̂ is near 0 or 1, or when n is moderate. The Wilson score interval has better coverage in these regimes and avoids the embarrassment of CIs that extend below 0 or above 1. We report Wilson intervals on every cell rate.



Cell-level escape rates with Wilson intervals


Show code
micro_profile <- co |>
  filter(size == "Micro", governance != "Other") |>
  mutate(
    legal_short = case_when(
      str_detect(legal_nature, "LLC")                 ~ "LLC",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Sole Proprietorship") ~ "Sole Proprietary",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Individual Limited")  ~ "EIRELI",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Simple")              ~ "Simple Partnership",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Silent")              ~ "Silent Partnership",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Corporation")         ~ "Corporation",
      TRUE                                            ~ "Other"
    )
  )

# Wilson score interval for a binomial proportion
wilson_ci <- function(k, n, conf = 0.95) {
  z  <- qnorm(1 - (1 - conf) / 2)
  p  <- k / n
  lo <- (p + z^2 / (2 * n) -
           z * sqrt(p * (1 - p) / n + z^2 / (4 * n^2))) / (1 + z^2 / n)
  hi <- (p + z^2 / (2 * n) +
           z * sqrt(p * (1 - p) / n + z^2 / (4 * n^2))) / (1 + z^2 / n)
  tibble(ci_lo = lo, ci_hi = hi)
}

escape_rate <- micro_profile |>
  group_by(legal_short, governance) |>
  summarise(
    n_total    = n(),
    n_rich     = sum(is_capital_rich_micro),
    escape_pct = n_rich / n_total,
    .groups    = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(n_total >= 20) |>
  mutate(
    wilson_ci(n_rich, n_total),                    # splices ci_lo, ci_hi
    combo_label = paste0(legal_short, " × ",
                         str_replace(governance, "\\n", " "))
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(escape_pct))

escape_rate |>
  select(legal_short, governance, n_total, n_rich,
         escape_pct, ci_lo, ci_hi) |>
  mutate(governance = str_replace(governance, "\n", " ")) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Capital-rich micro escape rates by legal form × governance**"),
    subtitle = "Wilson 95% confidence intervals · cells with n < 20 excluded"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    legal_short = "Legal form",
    governance  = "Governance archetype",
    n_total     = "N",

Independence test: are legal form and capital-rich status linked?
A formal omnibus check before the visual: pooling across governance, is the legal-form distribution different among capital-rich micros than among non-capital-rich micros? If the χ² test fails to reject, the cell-level pattern might be noise. If it rejects, the cells are carrying real structure.

  pivot_wider(names_from  = is_capital_rich_micro,

              values_fill = 0,
              names_prefix = "rich_") |>

  column_to_rownames("legal_short") |>

Visualization: the ranked escape profile


Show code

  escape_rate,
  aes(x = fct_reorder(combo_label, escape_pct),
      y = escape_pct,
      fill = governance)
) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7, alpha = 0.95) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = ci_lo, ymax = ci_hi),
                width = 0.18, colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.55) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(escape_pct, accuracy = 0.1),
                y = ci_hi),
            hjust = -0.18, size = 3.3, colour = "grey20") +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0("n = ", comma(n_total)), y = 0.003),
            hjust = 0, size = 2.8, colour = "white", fontface = "bold") +
  coord_flip(clip = "off") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(),
    limits = c(0, 0.62),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05)),
    name   = "Share of cell above the 75th capital percentile (BRL 500,000)"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal_gov, name = "Governance archetype",
                    guide = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)) +
  labs(
    title    = "The micro-enterprise escape profile is a partnership with a passive equity holder",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Share of capital-rich micros (>BRL 500k) by legal form × governance cell. ",
      "Error bars = Wilson 95% CI.\n",
      "Cells with n < 20 excluded. Counts shown in white."
    ),
    x        = NULL,
    caption  = caption_text
  ) +
  theme_cwc() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y        = element_text(size = 10, colour = "grey20"),
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 40, 10, 10)
  )








Figure 4.1: Capital-rich-micro share by legal form × governance combination.







Interpretation
Ranking the cells that survive the n ≥ 20 cutoff produces a clean ordering.
Simple Partnership × Beneficial Owner sits at the top (~48%). Almost half of the micros in this cell are capital-rich. The Wilson interval excludes any rate below the cell-pooled mean; the result is not a small-sample artifact.
Simple Partnership × Managing Partner is second (~42%). The same legal form, with an active rather than passive controlling party, still produces well-above- average escape rates. The legal form itself is doing work, independent of who controls it.
Sole Proprietary × Sole Entrepreneur, LLC × Managing Partner, EIRELI × Beneficial Owner trail behind (in the low-30s and below). EIRELI in particular is not the dominant escape route — its rate is below the LLC × Managing Partner cell. The popular “EIRELI as escape vehicle” framing is not what the data show.
The pattern is a two-dimensional combination, not a one-dimensional channel:

Partnership law is doing structural work. The Simple Partnership is a multi-party form requiring two or more named parties on paper. Its presence at the top of the ranking — across both governance archetypes — suggests that the partnership-form requirement of multiple registered parties opens a channel for capital that single-person forms (EIRELI, Sole Proprietorship) cannot match, even when those single-person forms permit comparable nominal capital.
Passive ownership amplifies the channel. Within the Simple Partnership form, switching from Managing Partner to Beneficial Owner control adds about six percentage points to the escape rate. The same legal architecture, more capital, when the controlling party is passive equity rather than active operator.

The combined signature — partnership law plus passive equity — is the fingerprint of holding-company architecture operating through Brazil’s small-business legal infrastructure: a passive equity holder channels capital into a nominally multi-party vehicle whose annual revenue happens to sit below the Simples threshold. The vehicle is legally a micro-enterprise; structurally it is a subsidiary.
crumbs: 4  Capital Without Category — Part III

title: Capital Without Category — Part III
text:
Methodological Notes

Why not logistic regression here. An earlier draft estimated a logistic model regressing capital-rich status on governance and legal form with reference categories. The cell-level visualization communicates the same structural finding more directly because it makes the interaction between legal form and governance visible — which is the substantive result. A logistic model with main effects only would average across the interaction; a model with the full interaction would produce 12 coefficients no easier to read than the cells themselves.
Why the 75th percentile cutoff. The threshold is descriptive, not normative. We tested 80th and 90th percentile cutoffs as sensitivity; the ranking of cells is stable, though absolute rates of course shift down with a stricter threshold.
What we cannot say. This is a descriptive analysis of administrative registry data. We are not estimating a treatment effect. The capital-rich micros may be choosing partnership form because they have capital (selection on capacity) rather than acquiring capital because they chose partnership form (legal-form-as-channel). Distinguishing these requires panel data or a registration-reform discontinuity, neither of which the CNPJ snapshot supports.
crumbs: 4  Capital Without Category — Part III

title: Capital Without Category — Part III
text:
Synthesis: The Trilogy
Three findings link up into a single argument about the limits of revenue-based firm classification.
Part I — concentration is universal. Capital concentration in Brazilian formal-sector firms is a power-law structure shared by every size tier (Gini > 0.98 everywhere; top-1% share of 96–99%). Treating “micro-enterprise” as a uniquely unequal category misreads the data. The same shape sits behind every regulatory label.
Part II — governance is distributional. Governance archetype shapes the capital distribution differently across types: Managing Partners deliver a level premium that scales with size, Sole Entrepreneurs stay flat (consistent with the agency-theory prediction for solo proprietors), and Beneficial Owners produce a heavy-tail distribution where most firms are small and a minority hold enormous capital. Size and governance are orthogonal dimensions of the registry, and treating them as substitutes systematically misallocates.
Part III — the escape route is structural. Among micro-enterprises, the capital-rich subpopulation concentrates in Simple Partnership × Beneficial Owner and Simple Partnership × Managing Partner cells — partnership law plus equity ownership rather than the popular EIRELI framing. This is the fingerprint of holding-company architecture operating through small-business legal infrastructure.
The policy implication is direct. If Brazil’s development goal is to support genuinely capital-constrained entrepreneurs, revenue alone is a porous targeting instrument. The registry already carries the information needed to do better: legal form and governance archetype are recorded for every entity at registration, both available without survey or audit cost. A targeting rule that conditions support on the combination — small revenue, single-party legal form, sole or active governance — would exclude the partnership-with- passive-equity escape route without imposing additional reporting burden on the firms most policy is meant to reach.
The deeper structural point is the same one the analysis opened with. Categories built on a single dimension (here, revenue) cannot do the work of multi-dimensional structure (here, legal form × governance × revenue). When the real heterogeneity is multi-dimensional and the targeting tool is one- dimensional, the tool fails on its own terms — and the failure is not random noise but a systematic bias toward the architecturally sophisticated.
crumbs: 4  Capital Without Category — Part III

title: Appendix A — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
Project Description
Brazil’s official firm-size classification (Simples Nacional)(https://thebrazilbusiness.com/article/taxation-regime-simples-nacional) sorts registered companies into micro, small, and medium/large tiers based on annual gross revenue. But revenue may be a poor proxy for capital endowment. A sole proprietor earning 360,000 BRL per year is legally a “micro-enterprise” regardless of whether they hold 10,000 BRL or 10,000,000 BRL in declared capital stock. This raises what might be called a classification paradox: the same regulatory label could encompass both subsistence operations and well-capitalized investment vehicles.
This project uses Brazil’s CNPJ business registry(Cadastro Nacional da Pessoa Jurídica–National Registry of Legal Entities) (https://dados.gov.br/dados/conjuntos-dados/cadastro-nacional-da-pessoa-juridica—cnpj) — a near-census of all registered firms — to expose the internal heterogeneity that aggregate size categories conceal. We focus on two orthogonal dimensions that revenue-based classification ignores: within-tier capital inequality and governance structure (the legal role of the controlling owner). Our central question is whether “micro-enterprise” functions as a coherent economic category or a regulatory label that shelters firms with vastly different capital endowments under the same umbrella — with direct implications for how development policy should target capital support.
crumbs: A  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

title: Appendix A — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
Research Questions

Within-tier inequality: To what extent do capital endowments vary among micro-enterprises within the same regulatory tier? How does within-tier capital inequality, measured by the Gini coefficient, compare across firm-size classifications?
Governance as a capital predictor: How does owner qualification — Managing Partner, Beneficial Owner, or Sole Entrepreneur — relate to capital accumulation independently of firm size? To what extent does a governance premium persist within the same regulatory tier?
The “capital-rich micro”: What combinations of legal form and governance archetype characterize micro-enterprises with unusually high declared capital stock? What does this pattern suggest about how development programs might better identify genuinely capital-constrained firms?
crumbs: A  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

title: Appendix A — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
Dataset
Brazilian Companies (CNPJ)
The dataset is drawn from Brazil’s Cadastro Nacional da Pessoa Jurídica (CNPJ), the federal business registry administered by the Receita Federal (Federal Revenue Service). It contains registration records for firms across Brazil with the following key variables:
The dataset is publicly available via the TidyTuesday GitHub repository and requires no restricted access or IRB approval | Variable | Type | Description | |—|—|—| | capital_social | Numeric (BRL) | Declared capital stock at registration | | porte_empresa | Categorical | Firm size tier under Simples Nacional (Micro, Small, Medium/Large) | | natureza_juridica | Categorical | Legal form (LLC, sole proprietorship, EIRELI, etc.) | | owner_qualification | Categorical | Legal role of controlling owner (Managing Partner, Beneficial Owner, Sole Entrepreneur, Other) | | municipio | Categorical | Municipality of registration | | cnae_fiscal | Categorical | Primary sector code (CNAE classification) |
The working subset used in this project contains 141,332 observations after standard cleaning (removal of records with missing or zero capital stock, harmonization of legal-form codes). Capital stock is right-skewed and will be log-transformed for distributional analysis. The owner_qualification field is recoded into four governance archetypes: Managing Partner / Administrator, Sole Entrepreneur, Beneficial Owner (passive/investor), and Other.

title: Appendix A — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
Inspiration
This project grew out of a prior TidyTuesday analysis that identified a macro-level capital paradox: micro-enterprises constitute the majority of registered Brazilian firms yet control a disproportionately small share of total capital stock. That finding raises a harder question — is this gap primarily between size tiers, within them, or both simultaneously?
The answer matters for development policy. If the capital deficit is concentrated among a genuine subset of micro-enterprises while others are actually well-capitalized holding-company subsidiaries registered as “micro” because their revenue falls below the Simples Nacional threshold, then blanket micro-enterprise subsidies are a blunt and potentially misdirected instrument. This project is motivated by the broader development economics question of when categorical targeting works and when it fails — a question with relevance well beyond Brazil, extending to informal economy support programs across Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and Latin America.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text: Case Study: Uber Is Taking Millions Of Manhattan Rides Away From Taxis

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
Source
Fischer-Baum, Reuben, and Carl Bialik. 2015.
Uber Is Taking Millions Of Manhattan Rides Away From Taxis.
FiveThirtyEight.
https://fivethirtyeight.com/features/uber-is-taking-millions-of-manhattan-rides-away-from-taxis/

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
What is the data story?
The Story the article revealed is that Uber’s growth in New York City did not simply add more total rides on top of the existing taxi system. However, in Manhattan especially, Uber appears to have taken millions of rides away from taxis. The article expressed a common concern that Uber was directly increasing congestion by showing that many of Uber’s gains were matched by taxi losses.}

title: Appendix B — Case Study
section: How the report unfolds the story
text:
How the report unfolds the story
The report unfolds in a very efficient way. First, the title and subtitle state the structure of the story very clearly, so readers can understand what the article is going to talk about. Then the article gives political and policy context by explaining concerns about congestion and the debate over Uber’s impact in New York City. This leads to why this analysis matters.
Secondly, the story introduces a simple comparison of pickup counts between years 2014 and 2015. Very easy to understand concept: Uber pickups rise sharply, yellow taxi pickups fall, and the total number of pickups changes only slightly. So the demand for ride is still there. Then the first chart works as the foundation of the entire story because it introduces the idea of substitution rather than pure growth in rides.
After that, the article becomes more detailed by asking where the changes happened. The maps are important because they move the argument from a citywide summary to a geographic explanation. They show that in Manhattan’s core area, Uber gains closely match taxi losses, while in outer boroughs both services sometimes increase. This adds spatial depth to the claim and helps readers see that Uber’s role differs by location.
Then the report then uses a scatterplot to make the strongest analytical point relationship wise. By comparing the change in Uber pickups to the change in taxi pickups across Manhattan taxi zones, the visualization shows a strong negative relationship. This supports the idea that Uber was replacing taxi rides on something close to a one-to-one basis in many places.
Finally, the article includes limitations and exceptions. It explains that pickup counts do not capture the full picture of congestion because they do not include trip length, dropoffs, or all competing services. That ending is effective because it strengthens credibility. The article still makes a strong claim, but it avoids pretending the data proves more than it actually can.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
What is effective?
The narrative structure of the story is very strong. It does not just present a collection of charts. Instead, it starts with a broad claim, gives context, presents simple evidence first, then gradually builds toward more detailed and convincing visual support. That makes the story very easy to follow.
Another strength is the progression of visual complexity. The report begins with a basic comparison chart, then moves to maps, and finally to a more analytical scatterplot. This sequence helps readers build understanding step by step instead of overwhelming them all at once.
The visualizations are also tightly connected to the argument. Each one answers a specific question. The early chart answers whether total pickups increased much. The maps answer where the changes happened. The scatterplot answers how closely Uber gains matched taxi losses. Because every figure has a purpose, the report feels focused and persuasive.
A final strength is that the article balances confidence with caution. It makes a strong argument, but it also acknowledges what the data cannot show. That makes the overall story more trustworthy.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
What could be improved?
One limitation is that the article want to show causation but is limited to only correlation. The evidence strongly suggests that Uber replaced taxi rides, but the report cannot fully prove that Uber alone caused the taxi decline.
Another weakness is that pickup counts are only a partial measure of congestion. Congestion depends on more than just how many rides begin. It also depends on trip length, driving patterns between fares, dropoff locations, and the number of other vehicles on the road. The article admits this, but it is still an important limitation.
The report could also be improved by including a longer time span. Comparing only 2014 and 2015 gives a useful snapshot, but a multi-year trend would make the argument even stronger and help show whether this was a short-term shift or a stable long-term pattern.
Finally, some readers could find the scatterplot harder to interpret than the earlier charts. It is analytically strong, but it requires more statistical reading skill than the simpler visuals.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
Takeaway
This case study is a strong example of data storytelling because it combines a clear claim, purposeful visualizations, and a logical progression of evidence. The article does not simply show data; it guides the reader through a sequence of questions and answers. That structure makes the final conclusion persuasive: in Manhattan, Uber appears to have replaced many taxi rides rather than substantially increasing the total number of pickups. This is a useful model for building our own project narrative, where charts should appear in an order that helps readers move from basic context to deeper interpretation.

title: Appendix C — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
C.1 Background and Motivation
Brazil’s official firm-size classification (Simples Nacional)(https://thebrazilbusiness.com/article/taxation-regime-simples-nacional) sorts registered companies into micro, small, and medium/large tiers based on annual gross revenue. But revenue is a poor proxy for capital endowment. A sole proprietor earning 360,000 BRL per year is legally a “micro-enterprise” regardless of whether they hold 10,000 BRL or 10,000,000 BRL in declared capital stock. This classification logic creates a structural paradox: the same regulatory label simultaneously shelters subsistence operations and well-capitalized investment vehicles.
This project uses Brazil’s CNPJ(Cadastro Nacional da Pessoa Jurídica – National Registry of Legal Entities) business registry(https://dados.gov.br/dados/conjuntos-dados/cadastro-nacional-da-pessoa-juridica—cnpj) — a near-census of all registered firms — to expose the internal heterogeneity that aggregate size categories conceal. We focus on two orthogonal dimensions that revenue-based classification ignores:
Our central argument is that “micro-enterprise” is not a coherent economic category for capital analysis — and that development policy instruments calibrated to revenue thresholds alone will systematically misidentify their target population.

Within-tier capital inequality: How unequal is the capital distribution inside each size tier, and how much of total inequality is within versus between tiers?
Governance structure: Does the legal role of the controlling owner (Managing Partner, Beneficial Owner, Sole Entrepreneur) predict capital accumulation independently of size, and does this governance premium vary across the capital distribution?

title: Appendix C — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
C.2 Research Questions

Within-tier inequality: Are micro-enterprises internally unequal in capital endowments? How do bootstrapped Gini coefficients compare across size tiers, and what share of total inequality is attributable to between-tier versus within-tier variation?
Governance as a capital predictor: Does owner qualification predict capital accumulation independently of firm size? Is the governance premium uniform across the capital distribution, or does it widen or narrow at the tails?
The “capital-rich micro”: Among micro-enterprises above the 75th capital percentile, what legal form-governance combinations most strongly predict capital-rich status? What are the odds ratios from a logistic model of capital-rich membership?

title: Appendix C — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
C.3 Data and Setup

Packages


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(scales)
library(ggridges)
library(patchwork)
library(boot)          # bootstrap Gini confidence intervals
library(quantreg)      # quantile regression
library(broom)         # tidy model outputs
library(gt)            # publication-quality tables
library(modelsummary)  # regression summary tables
library(ggtext)        # markdown / HTML in ggplot labels
library(glue)          # string interpolation

# ── Shared palette ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
pal_size <- c("Micro" = "#e76f51", "Small" = "#f4a261", "Medium/Large" = "#264653")
pal_gov  <- c(
  "Sole Entrepreneur"                    = "#e9c46a",
  "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator"    = "#4a90d9",
  "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)" = "#264653"
)

caption_base <- "Source: Brazilian CNPJ Registry ·He, Miao & Boyd"




Data Ingestion and Cleaning


Code
companies <- read_csv(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2026/2026-01-27/companies.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

# ── Core recoding ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
co <- companies |>
  # Drop records with missing or non-positive capital (not usable for log-scale analysis)
  filter(
    !is.na(company_size),
    !is.na(capital_stock),
    capital_stock > 0,
    !is.na(owner_qualification)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    # Size tier (ordered factor)
    size = case_when(
      company_size == "micro-enterprise" ~ "Micro",
      company_size == "small-enterprise" ~ "Small",
      TRUE                               ~ "Medium/Large"
    ),
    size = factor(size, levels = c("Micro", "Small", "Medium/Large")),

    # Governance archetype: collapse owner_qualification into 4 classes
    governance = case_when(
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("beneficial owner", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~
        "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)",
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("entrepreneur|business owner", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~
        "Sole Entrepreneur",
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("managing partner|administrator", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~
        "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    governance = factor(governance, levels = c(
      "Sole Entrepreneur",
      "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)",
      "Other"
    )),

    # Log10 capital for distributional analysis
    log_capital = log10(capital_stock),

    # Legal-form shorthand for micro analysis
    legal_short = case_when(
      str_detect(legal_nature, "LLC")                 ~ "LLC",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Sole Proprietorship") ~ "Sole Prop.",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Individual Limited")  ~ "EIRELI",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Simple")              ~ "Simple Part.",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Silent")              ~ "Silent Part.",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Corporation")         ~ "Corporation",
      TRUE                                            ~ "Other"
    ),

    # Capital-rich flag: top quartile *within* micro-enterprises
    capital_q75_micro     = quantile(capital_stock[size == "Micro"], 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    is_capital_rich_micro = (size == "Micro") & (capital_stock >= capital_q75_micro)
  )

cat("Final observations:", scales::comma(nrow(co)), "\n")

title: Appendix C — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
C.4 Synthesis
This analysis reframes the Brazilian micro-enterprise capital paradox across four dimensions.
1. The trap operates at two scales simultaneously. The Theil T decomposition shows that a majority of total capital inequality is within size tiers, not between them. Revenue-based classification reform cannot close the capital gap, because the gap is not primarily a between-tier phenomenon.
2. Governance is an independent, orthogonal capital predictor. The nested OLS models confirm that incorporating governance structure adds statistically significant explanatory power beyond size alone. The governance premium is not a proxy for size.
3. The premium is not uniform: it widens at the upper tail. Quantile regression at τ ∈ {0.10, …, 0.90} shows that the Beneficial Owner advantage over sole entrepreneurs grows substantially at higher quantiles. OLS estimates the conditional mean, which masks this distributional asymmetry entirely. The upper tail of the micro-enterprise capital distribution is disproportionately occupied by Beneficial Owner-controlled firms resembling holding-company subsidiaries, not organic small businesses.
4. Capital-rich micro status is predictable from governance and legal form. The logistic model confirms that Beneficial Ownership and LLC legal form are the strongest independent predictors of capital-rich status within the micro tier, with odds ratios significantly above 1 after controlling for both dimensions simultaneously. Sole Entrepreneurs in Sole Proprietorships — the most structurally exposed population — have the lowest probability of capital-rich status and the highest probability of genuine capital constraint.
Policy implication. If Brazil’s development goal is to direct capital support toward genuinely constrained small entrepreneurs, the targeting instrument should integrate governance structure alongside revenue-based size classification. The owner_qualification field is already present in CNPJ administrative records at zero additional data collection cost. Adding it as a screening variable would substantially improve the precision of capital-support programs. Revenue thresholds are necessary but insufficient — and this dataset shows precisely why.
